title: Redirect Semantics, Method Rewriting, Credential Scope, and Loop Control · Browser and Node Client Systems · TypeScript Build Room
summary: An accessibility product team integrating a staging browser API must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for redirect semantics, method rewriting, credential scope, and loop control, producing auditable redirect policy engine. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch auditable redirect policy engine while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory,…
objectives: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Resolve Location references against the current request URL and validate the resulting destination each hop. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Bound redirect hops, detect loops, retain the chain, and expose the policy decision to callers. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Redirect Status Semantics
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK sends POST T-2 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Redirect Status Semantics.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Redirect Status Semantics, keep Redirect Status Semantics connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.text: accessible staging dashboard SDK sends POST T-2 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

options.text: State the Redirect Status Semantics preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.

All redirect status codes preserve the original method and body. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch. That claim does not survive the commit to the mission model evidence boundary.

For commit to the mission model, run one convenient Redirect Status Semantics happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Audit the initial evidence packet · Workshop Manual Redirect Defense
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK sends POST T-3 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Manual Redirect Defense happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Manual Redirect Defense condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.
